text: The surgeon dissects the area after opening the gastrocolic ligament and the epiplomb's electrocavity, carefully preserving the Frédé band to dissociate and release station number six of the gastroepiploic vessels on the right.

The surgeon performs dissection by smoothing, abundantly opening the gastrocolic ligament. In this corpulent patient, the anatomical planes are more easily recognized, aided by the insufflation of gas. Gentle maneuvers are executed to avoid cutting the transverse mesocolon.

The surgeon isolates the right gastrointestinal vein along with the Henle trunk, which is visible below and must be preserved. Clips are applied to ligate and section the vein, allowing for the dissection of the gastro-bibloid vessels in a single block. The gastro-bibloid artery is then dissected, and during the dissection of the periapital space, a small leakage is observed and controlled using a bipolar clamp.

The surgeon attempts to coagulate the orifice but fails, leading to an erroneous attempt to blindly clip and dissect the artery. This results in a hemorrhage that obscures the camera view. The surgical team cleans the camera and re-enters the field to locate the source of bleeding.

The surgeon identifies the bleeding source beneath the clamp and uses suction to clean the surgical field, improving visibility. With the left hand, the surgeon locates and closes the bleeding hole, allowing for better identification of any potential leakage.

The surgeon approaches the bleeding site and performs washing to identify the hole in the right gastrointestinal artery. A clip is placed on the artery, but bleeding persists, prompting the placement of a second clip nearby. The clamp is gently maneuvered to identify the highest position, allowing the continuation of the surgical intervention with the section of the gastroepiploic artery near the duodenal gastro, followed by the dissection of the duodenum.

The surgeon places gauze for hemostatic control and anatomical landmarks. The iliopathic region is identified, showing lymphangitis, possibly neoplastic or fibrotic, in the fat cells near the liver. Traction and counter-traction maneuvers are used to identify the main biliary structure.

The surgeon releases the common hepatic artery, which is looped with a colored vessel loop for identification and mobilization. Adjacent to it, the main biliary duct is identified and released by dissecting the cystic duct near its origin, leaving the cystic cord en bloc in the operative field. The anatomy is clarified, and a duodenal section is performed to move the operative part away from the working field.

The surgeon identifies the right hepatic artery, which courses laterally to the common hepatic duct. Station XIII is visible laterally to the duct, and the surgeon isolates and dissects this area. The biliary tract is observed with tenacious adhesions to the right hepatic artery.

The surgeon performs gentle dissection maneuvers to remove tissue closely connected to the common hepatic duct. The common hepatic duct, along with the right and left hepatic ducts, is identified. A vessel loop is used to underpass the main bile duct, revealing the anatomy including the common hepatic duct, right hepatic artery, and portal branch. Station XIII emerges as the dissection progresses along the Smussa road.

The surgeon attempts to isolate station number thirteen using ultrasonic dissection, focusing on the tip of the station. During the procedure, station thirteen is brought towards itself, revealing an aberrant hepatic accessory emerging from the common bile duct.

The surgeon identifies a modest leakage and attempts to suture it using two Prolene 6-0 stitches. The aberrant duct is closed at the sinus level in the choledochus and is excised for histological examination. The leakage appears well-defined, and the right branch is highlighted, following its normal anatomical course.

The surgeon performs a regional esophagus anastomosis, isolating the esophagus by seven to eight centimeters. A gastrointestinal tube guides the procedure to ensure proper penetration and secure mucosal suturing for the anastomosis.

The surgeon positions a linear suture on the right side of the esophagus using a 45mm stapler. The esophagus is partially transected, revealing a challenge as the probe becomes trapped, pulling the anastomotic block towards the mediastinum. The surgeon attempts to locate the probe visually and by palpation, noting its entrapment at the upper margin. The suture is completed, and the anesthesiologist assists in removing the probe, revealing two clips from the mechanical suturing. The probe is repositioned to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon perfects the esophagus and digunal esophagus anastomosis using two continuous sutures with a 3-0 monofilament. The right margin is sutured with a seromuscular technique, consolidating the esophagus in the intrathoracic position. The remaining esophagus is cut, and a second suture line is completed on the left side, ensuring the seromuscular layer is secured and the suture line is closed.
